title: C Union
content:
### Union in C

A union is similar to a structure, but all members share the same memory location. Only one member can hold a value at a time.

### Definition

```c
union Data {
  int i;
  float f;
  char str[20];
};
```

### Union vs Structure

| Feature | Structure | Union |
|---------|-----------|-------|
| Memory | Sum of all members | Size of largest member |
| Member access | All members available | Only one member at a time |
| Use case | Group related data | Store one of several types |
| Alignment | Each member has its own space | All members share same space |

### Memory Layout

For the `union Data` above:
- `sizeof(union Data)` = 20 bytes (size of `char[20]`, the largest member)
- `i`, `f`, and `str` all start at the same memory address

### Use Cases

- Storing different types in the same memory location
- Implementing variant types
- Type punning (interpreting bytes as different types)
- Saving memory when only one type is used at a time

---

> **Key Point:** Writing to one union member overwrites the others. Accessing a member that was not the last one written leads to undefined behavior.

quiz.question: How much memory does a union allocate?
